input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies and a multi‑notation modelling environment. The core architectural description is specified with SysML, utilising component‑connector diagrams and state‑machine blocks. For finer behavioural modelling MontiArc (including MontiArc Automaton) and SysML v1.7 state machine extensions are employed. Simulation of the logical model is conducted in MathWorks Simulink, while algorithmic components are coded in C++ or Java. The data integration layer relies on conventional database technologies (e.g. SQL‑based repositories), and the twin is hosted on a software platform that supports these languages and tools. Together, these enablers provide a cohesive development pipeline for model, simulate, and code the digital counterpart of the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation employs a multi‑layered Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach. Structural and component relationships are captured with SysML (v1.7) and ADLs such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, using component‑connector diagrams to link functionalities, interfaces and composition. For behavioral modeling, MontiArc Automaton (state‑machine based) and SysML state‑machine diagrams describe control logic, while analytic relationships are represented in SysML mathematical constraints.  The functional and logical architecture is further refined with the RFLP (Requirements‑Functions‑Logics‑Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT methodologies to partition the system into logical, functional and product layers.  Simulation of the dynamic behavior is performed with MathWorks Simulink, which can generate executable models in C++ or Java for integration with embedded software.  Data sources span design models, simulation outputs, and compiled code artifacts, managed through a heterogeneous modeling ecosystem that maintains model relationships via connectors, data maps, and version control.  The combined computational models and data serve to describe the system’s physical form, operational behavior, and enable predictive simulation, data‑driven analysis, and integration with real‑time sensor feeds in the Digital Twin lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows the typical MBSE multi‑layered abstraction hierarchy. Development begins with a Requirements layer that captures functional and performance constraints. Progressing to a Functional Architecture layer, SysML Interfaces and Block Definition Diagrams (BDDs) describe the component and connector structure, as well as the deterministic behavior via SysML state machines. A Logical Architecture layer refines the behavior into executable models, often represented by MontiArc Automata or Simulink block‑diagram models, providing a bridge between specification and implementation. The Product/Technical Architecture layer contains the physical system description and hardware‑software mapping, for instance using AADL component specifications or AADL architecture. Transitions between layers are managed through transformation rules that preserve semantics and map SysML diagrams to ADLs, allowing traceability and re‑use. Lifecycle governance is addressed implicitly by the heterogeneous stakeholder integration requirement, requiring version control, model‑library management and architecture reviews at each abstraction transition.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin implementation follows OMG standards such as SysML v1.7 for functional and logical architecture modelling, UML for class diagrams, and the Architecture Analysis and Design Language (AADL) for component‑level specification. These standards provide interoperability and traceability across the development life‑cycle.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
